fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up that credential helper utility library we've been talking about. The core idea is that right now every time someone builds a new credential helper they're re-writing the same fiddly bits from scratch — parsing Git's wire format, figuring out config values, building auth headers, etc. We keep getting bug reports that boil down to someone getting the casing rules wrong or messing up the encoding.

Basically we need a clean multi-file library (not one giant file please, we had that problem last time with the login module) that handles all these building blocks in one place. Things like interpreting config strings the way Git does, chopping strings around delimiters, encoding credentials for headers, reading and writing those key=value blocks Git sends over stdin/stdout, and reconstructing URLs from the query fields Git passes in.

The tricky part is the library itself shouldn't know anything about JSON or how we're testing it — there should be a separate thin adapter layer that handles that translation. Each feature should have proper error signals, not just generic crashes, and the errors should be clean category names, nothing with stack traces or internal type info leaking out.

I know Priya had some notes on the case-sensitivity rules for config keys that we should follow — can someone track those down? Also need to make sure the URL scope-walking logic matches what Git actually does when it walks remotes. Timeline is pretty tight so let's make sure we nail the edge cases on the boolean parsing and the base64 encoding stuff first.

One more thing from the questions that came in: for Git-style booleans, we should treat the accepted spellings as a fixed case-insensitive set. Truthy is exactly true, yes, on, 1 and falsey is exactly false, no, off, 0. Anything else should come back as 'none' — that includes empty string, whitespace-only strings, and null/absent values. So the library should report the three-state result as 'true', 'false', or 'none'.

Also found the extra detail on config key matching. A configuration key has the form section[.scope].property. The section segment before the first dot and the property segment after the last dot are compared case-insensitively, but the scope segment in the middle is compared case-sensitively. So 'foo.example.com.bar' and 'FOO.example.com.BAR' are equal, but 'foo.example.com.bar' and 'foo.EXAMPLE.COM.bar' are not. Same rule everywhere we do Feature 4 key comparisons: section and property are case-insensitive, scope is case-sensitive.

On error handling, let's keep it really strict and boring: errors come back as one normalized line only, in the format 'error=<category>' and nothing else. The categories we need are 'argument_null' for a required string input that is absent or null, 'out_of_range' for an integer that fails a range constraint, 'unknown_op' for an unrecognized operation, and 'invalid_input' for other invalid input conditions. We should never leak host-language type names, stack traces, or runtime-generated message text into that output.

For the URL scope walking, the order is most specific to least specific. Start by unfolding the path right-to-left one component at a time, and make sure query strings and fragments are dropped and trailing slashes are trimmed. After that, unfold the host left-to-right by removing one leading sub-domain at a time, and stop before emitting a bare top-level domain, meaning a host with no dots. For example, 'http://buzz.foo.example.com/bar/baz' produces five scopes: the full URL with path, then with just /bar, then host-only, then foo.example.com, then example.com.

And for the URL-safe base64 bit, byte value 62 maps to '-' and byte value 63 maps to '_' instead of the standard '+' and '/'. If padding is enabled, keep trailing '=' so the output length is a multiple of four. If padding is disabled, strip all trailing '='. An empty input produces an empty output string.

trigger_keywords: should the library know about JSON
how is the adapter separated from core logic
what does the adapter layer do
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core library must have no knowledge of JSON, stdin/stdout, or the 'op' dispatch field. All core utilities must be callable directly through an idiomatic API. Only the execution adapter layer translates JSON commands into calls to the core API and renders results back to stdout. The core logic must be organized into multiple files by concern — config value semantics, string slicing, key comparison, URL/scope derivation, encoding, stream dictionary I/O, credential-query parsing, argument validation — and must not be a single file.

trigger_keywords: how is the dict format terminated
what line endings are accepted when reading key value pairs
how are duplicate keys handled when reading a dict
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dictionary format is a sequence of 'key=value' lines terminated by a blank line. Both LF and CRLF line endings are accepted on read. The split into key and value occurs at the first '=' so values may themselves contain '=' characters. Leading and trailing spaces and tabs are preserved verbatim in both keys and values. By default keys are case-sensitive (so 'a' and 'A' are distinct); when case-insensitive matching is requested a later key overwrites an earlier one differing only in case, and the earlier key's casing is preserved.

trigger_keywords: how is the remote URI reconstructed from credential fields
when is the username included in the URI
what happens to the password when building the URI
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The remote URI requires both protocol and host fields; if either is missing no URI is produced and the output is 'uri=<none>'. The host may carry a port as 'host:port' which is preserved verbatim. The path, when present, is appended without an extra slash. By default the username is omitted from the URI. When user inclusion is explicitly requested, the username is URL-percent-encoded and embedded in the authority before the host (e.g., 'john.doe%40domain.com@example.com'). The password is never included in the URI under any circumstances.

trigger_keywords: how does string slicing handle a missing delimiter
what happens when the delimiter is not found
keep prefix or suffix when delimiter is absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the delimiter is not found in the string, the entire original string is returned unchanged — this applies to both keep_prefix and keep_suffix operations. A null/absent input string is a separate case that must be reported as 'error=argument_null'. An empty string input with no delimiter present is valid and returns an empty string result.

trigger_keywords: how is basic auth header formed
what encoding is used for the credential parameter
basic authentication scheme name in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Basic auth credential is formed by concatenating username + ':' + password, UTF-8 encoding the result, then applying standard (non-URL-safe) base64 encoding with padding. The output reports two fields: 'scheme=Basic' (always exactly this capitalization) and 'parameter=<base64 value>'. Empty username and/or empty password are supported — an empty username with a password produces ':password' before encoding, and vice versa.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'dict_read', 'dict_write', 'credential_fields', 'field_lookup', 'remote_uri', 'host_port', 'range_check', 'git_boolean', 'booleany_or_default', 'keep_prefix', 'keep_suffix', 'remove_fragment', 'base64url', 'key_equals', 'key_order', 'concat_sequences', 'query_params', 'config_scopes', 'user_info', 'basic_auth_header'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the URL scope-walking logic matches what Git actually does when it walks remotes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 config_scopes logic in feature7_config_scopes.json: path unfolded right-to-left dropping query/fragment/trailing slashes, then host unfolded left-to-right removing sub-domains, stopping before bare TLD

context_pointers.vague_reference: not one giant file please, we had that problem last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: NFR #1 Scale-Driven Code Organization: the library must be split into multiple source files by concern — config semantics, string slicing, key comparison, URL/scope derivation, encoding, stream I/O, credential parsing, argument validation — with a separate execution/test adapter

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll want to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the feature descriptions in start.md to get the exact expected behavior for that scenario.

feature10_1_dict_read.json.description: Read key/value pairs from a text block formatted as 'key=value' lines terminated by a blank line. Both LF and CRLF line endings are accepted. The split occurs at the first '=' so values may themselves contain '='. Leading/trailing spaces and tabs are preserved verbatim as part of keys and values. By default keys are case-sensitive (so 'a' and 'A' are distinct entries); when case-insensitive matching is requested a later key overwrites an earlier one differing only in case. The output reports the entry count followed by each key=value pair.

feature10_2_dict_write.json.description: Serialize key/value pairs into a text block of 'key=value' lines followed by a terminating blank line. The line ending is selectable: 'lf' uses \n and 'crlf' uses \r\n. An empty set of entries produces just the terminating blank line. Spaces and tabs inside keys and values are written verbatim. The output is the serialized text block exactly as produced (the wire format is itself the contract).

feature11_1_credential_fields.json.description: Parse a set of credential-query arguments (a map of named fields) and expose the well-known fields: protocol, host, path, username, and password. A field that is present returns its value; a field that is absent is reported as unset.

feature11_2_field_lookup.json.description: Look up an arbitrary (non-well-known) field by name from a set of credential-query arguments. A present field returns its value; an absent field is reported as unset.

feature11_3_remote_uri.json.description: Reconstruct the remote repository URL from credential-query arguments. The URL requires both protocol and host; if either is missing no URL is produced. The host may carry a port as 'host:port'. The path, when present, is appended. By default the username is omitted; when user inclusion is requested the username is URL-encoded and embedded in the authority, but the password is never included.

feature11_4_host_port.json.description: Split the host field of a credential query into a hostname and optional numeric port. A host with no colon yields the hostname and no port. A host of the form 'name:number' yields the hostname and the parsed port. A host whose port portion is non-numeric is rejected as invalid. A missing host is also rejected as invalid.

feature12_range_check.json.description: Validate that an integer falls within a range defined by a lower and upper bound, where each bound can independently be inclusive or exclusive. A value satisfying the bounds is in range; a value violating either bound is reported as an out-of-range error. With both bounds inclusive, the endpoints themselves are accepted; making a bound exclusive rejects that endpoint.

feature1_1_boolean_classify.json.description: Classify a configuration string against Git's boolean conventions. Several spellings are equivalent to true (true/yes/on/1) and several to false (false/no/off/0), all case-insensitive; any other text (including empty, whitespace, or unset) is neither. Output reports whether the value is truthy, whether it is falsey, and the combined three-state interpretation (true, false, or none).

feature1_2_boolean_or_default.json.description: Interpret a configuration string as a boolean, but fall back to a caller-supplied default when the value is not a recognized boolean spelling. Recognized truthy spellings return true and recognized falsey spellings return false regardless of the default; any unrecognized value (including empty, whitespace, or unset) returns the provided default.

feature2_1_keep_prefix.json.description: Keep the portion of a string before a delimiter character, discarding the delimiter and everything after it. With anchor 'first', the cut is made at the first occurrence of the delimiter; with anchor 'last', the cut is made at the last occurrence. A string with no delimiter is returned unchanged. Passing an absent (null) string is an error reported as a null-argument condition.

feature2_2_keep_suffix.json.description: Keep the portion of a string after a delimiter, discarding the delimiter and everything before it. The delimiter may be a single character or a multi-character sequence. With anchor 'first' the cut is at the first occurrence; with anchor 'last' the cut is at the last occurrence. Matching is case-sensitive by default and case-insensitive when requested. A string that does not contain the delimiter is returned unchanged.

feature2_3_remove_fragment.json.description: Remove the first occurrence of a fragment from the middle of a string, splicing the surrounding text back together. Matching is case-sensitive by default and case-insensitive when requested. If the fragment is not found the string is returned unchanged.

feature3_base64url.json.description: Encode a byte array into the URL-safe base64 alphabet, where byte 62 is '-' and byte 63 is '_' instead of '+' and '/'. With padding enabled the output keeps trailing '=' padding to a multiple of four characters; with padding disabled the trailing '=' characters are removed. An empty input produces an empty string.

feature4_1_key_equals.json.description: Compare two configuration keys for equality. A key has the form section[.scope].property, where the optional scope is the middle portion between the first and last dots. The section and property segments compare case-insensitively, but the scope segment compares case-sensitively. Two unset (null) keys are considered equal; one unset and one set are not.

feature4_2_key_order.json.description: Order two configuration keys, returning the direction of the comparison: -1 if the first sorts before the second, 1 if after, and 0 if they are considered equal. Keys have the form section[.scope].property; section and property are compared case-insensitively while the scope is compared case-sensitively. Keys that compare equal under the equality rules order as 0.

feature5_concat_sequences.json.description: Concatenate any number of integer sequences left-to-right into a single sequence, preserving order and duplicate elements. The output reports the total element count and the flattened comma-separated sequence. A single sequence is returned as-is; multiple sequences are appended in the order given.

feature6_query_params.json.description: Parse the query-string portion of a URL into key/value pairs, URL-decoding both keys and values (so percent-encoded spaces and other characters are restored). The output reports the number of parameters followed by each decoded key=value pair.

cases.expected_output:
count=3
q1=value1
q2=value with spaces
key with spaces=value3

feature7_config_scopes.json.description: Given a remote URL, enumerate the ordered list of configuration scopes from most specific to least specific. The path is unfolded right-to-left one component at a time (query and fragment are dropped, trailing slashes trimmed), then the host is unfolded left-to-right by removing one leading sub-domain at a time, stopping before emitting a bare top-level domain. The output reports the count followed by each scope URL.

cases.expected_output:
count=5
http://buzz.foo.example.com/bar/baz
http://buzz.foo.example.com/bar
http://buzz.foo.example.com
http://foo.example.com
http://example.com

feature8_user_info.json.description: Extract the user-information component (username and optional password) embedded in a URL authority of the form scheme://username:password@host. Both fields are URL-decoded. When the URL carries no user info the result reports that none is present. When present, a missing password is reported as unset while an empty password is reported as an empty value; the password split occurs at the first colon so any later colons remain part of the password.

feature9_basic_auth_header.json.description: Build an HTTP Basic authentication header from a username and password. The credential is formed as 'username:password', UTF-8 encoded, and base64-encoded. The output reports the authentication scheme name and the encoded parameter value. Empty username and/or password are supported.
